author: 0xunclebill
content:
i have a few edge cases regarding payload attributes sse event and wondering if anyone else is coming across this. specifically serving the parent block number

edge case 1: if the non-finalized portion of the chain has no payload envelopes, we cant use fork choice to fetch the parent block number sice the latest finalized full block will have already been pruned from fc. we instead need to load it from the db by iterating back until we find the latest full block.

edge case 2: at the fork. at the fork block we fetch the parent block number from the pre-gloas block itself. if at the fork we have a missed payload envelope, we have to use fork choice to look at latest pre-gloas block to get the parent block number. and if we finalize gloas with zero envelopes, were back at edge case 1, but need some special logic to iterate back to the latest pre-gloas block.

probably other ways to solve this, but I think this is the most straightforward way to do it in lighthouse.

Its just feels like a lot of annoying edge cases to deal with. Do we really need to serve the parent block number? couldnt the EL derive that with just the parent block hash? it would make things easier for us. wondering what other clients think

author: 0xunclebill
content: oh michael wrote about this in the beacon api channel

author: 0xunclebill
content: sorry

content:

author: 0xunclebill
content: I believe VC needs to sign a request auth per builder post Gloas. so mev boost would need direct access to VC keys in order to multiplex correctly

author: 0xunclebill
content:
probably mev boost could be updated to support that. but seeing as over at least the past 24months its pretty much just been core devs maintaining mev-boost i dont see the point in putting more work into it. 

commit boost supports remote signing so they could probs support post-gloas functionality

author: nc1234
content: I think it has been poorly maintained. And I think Bharath's (omg I can't tag him anymore he left the server) argument is that Prysm has the ability to connect to single relay, then it should be trivial for clients to connect to multiple relays which is really all mev boost does. So instead of updating mev boost, might as well just deprecate it and let clients write their own multiplexer

content: it's easier to purge the jank if it's not part of CLs but some separate tool

author: vladimir_ea
content: I have a stupid question: the spec says that builders should publish execution payloads on the global `execution_payload` p2p gossip topic. Is the expectation that builders are themselves maintaining p2p gossip connections? Or is there a new api on beacon nodes that can be called? thanks.

author: vladimir_ea
content: what guarantees can a 'check for equivocation' provide? if i understand, beacon block attestations are produced at the 3 second mark and are then propagated - so is it the case that a builder can only guarantee there is no equivocation by waiting to observe a majority of attestations before broadcasting the payload?

author: vladimir_ea
content: if this is the case then it would be useful to builders to be able ot query a beacon node to discover if this point has been reached, rather than repeatly send a payload to the beascon node until this point is reached.

author: m.kalinin
content: great catch! `execution_payload_availability` should probably contain availability flag of the latest block for all empty slot

author: sproul
content:
yeah that seems like a good solution

another could be to use `slot_index = state.latest_block_header.slot % N`?

author: pk910
content:
a `broadcast_validation` flag leads to trying the submission multiple times as builders also don't wanna miss the reveal if the voting threshold gets hit at a later point?
doesn't it make more sense to listen to the attestation aggregates?  these are events on the SSE stream,  so the builder can keep track of the voting result and broadcast as soon as the threshold is reached.
That's the logic we've started implementing in buildoor.  It's just not fully wired up yet as we wanted some reliable reveal during testing

author: sproul
content:
there is no threshold and no waiting, the equivocation check is instantaneous at the time of publication.

if the beacon block has already been around more than 250ms without equivocation then IMO builders don’t need to wait for attesters and should just *send it* (and use the broadcast validation flag just for safety, and so they don’t need to bother about checking equivocations themselves or polling)

author: terencechain
content: nice catch! the bug is worse in the sense that every slot starts as "payload not yet avaialble" so we'll never compute the head reward correctly right?

author: m.kalinin
content: yeah this is another option, then during empty slots availability will basically be `0` which is true

author: sproul
content:
CC <@1257246783503994940> does this https://discord.com/channels/595666850260713488/874767108809031740/1519680816001716336 sound reasonable to you?

To elaborate a little more: shortly after the signed beacon block is received on gossip, you can be quite sure that it will remain canonical even if there's an equivocation (fork choice prefers the first received block). So after receiving the block containing the bid committing to your payload envelope, you send the payload envelope to the BN for publication with `broadcast_validation=consensus_and_equivocation`. IF the BN detects an equivocation then it won't publish, and worst case if the equivocation happens concurrently with the publication of your envelope, then the envelope _will_ be published, but it will most likely remain canonical (because its beacon block was received by the majority of the network prior to the equivocation)

author: sproul
content: the buggy logic only executes if there's a skipped slot, the head reward for a non-skipped slot (the common case) is fine

author: terencechain
content:
another option is to walk back like this, it's not nice so i dont recommend this:
```
 attested_slot = data.slot
          while attested_slot > 0 and (
              get_block_root_at_slot(state, Slot(attested_slot - 1)) == data.beacon_block_root
          ):
              attested_slot = Slot(attested_slot - 1)
          payload_index = state.execution_payload_availability[attested_slot % SLOTS_PER_HISTORICAL_ROOT]
```

author: sproul
content: yeah I think `state.latest_block_header.slot` while wrong in general, is correct in the case where the head matches, which is the only time the `payload_matches` value becomes relevant

author: vladimir_ea
content: can you unpack what is meant by 'detects equivocation' - this means a second different signed beacon block has been seen?

author: sproul
content: yes

author: sproul
content: we have a flag like this for beacon blocks already and it just checks if we've seen a block equivocation (nothing about waiting for a certain attestation weight), so I'm just extrapolating based on that

author: vladimir_ea
content:
if a proposer did publish 2 different beacon blocks, the order in which nodes would receive these beacon blocks via gossip is not determined is it? and if fork choice favours the first seen then depending on how these travel around the p2p network there is no saying which of the 2 would eventually be chosen - is that correct?

in this case a builder would be well advised to wait out any possible 'equivocation window' before publishing their payload - and almost certainly not to publish if a second beacon block is ever seen.

author: vladimir_ea
content: does anyone know how long it currently takes blocks to propagate through the consensus layer?

author: jtraglia
content: This might be the correct approach though. I have been testing this:

author: jtraglia
content:
Can you explain what you mean by "while wrong in general" here? Thoughts on the comment I left on github? Happy to chat here (it's easier).

> Hmm I don't think this would work. This would be the slot of the block containing the attestation, not the block being attested to. Because `process_block_header` will overwrite `state.latest_block_header.slot` and this is called before `process_operations` (which includes `process_attestation`).
